coredata.dc:title: Solvothermal synthesis of CDs/Bi4O5Br2 nanocomposites with improved visible-light photocatalytic ciprofloxacin (CIP) decontamination
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yaqian
Pan, Haoqi
Zhang, Fenge
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) and Bi-riched bismuth oxyhalide have great potential in the field of photocatalysis. Here, a series of CDs/Bi4O5Br2 at different loading ratios were successfully synthesized by solvothermal method. Experiments of visible light degradation of ciprofloxacin (CIP) showed that 3 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2 had the highest activity (5.39 times that of Bi4O5Br2) and the degradation rate reached 98%. The samples were characterized by XRD, SEM, TEM, EDS, Mapping, UV–vis DRS, Raman, and PL spectra. The absorption edge of 3 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2 was red-shifted from 455 nm to 580 nm compared with Bi4O5Br2. The enhancement of photocatalytic activity was mainly due to the fact that the addition of CDs not only broadened the photo-response range, but reduced the recombination rate of photo-induced carriers with good electron transport interfaces formed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon materials
Bi4O5Br2
Composite materials
Photocatalysis
Ciprofloxacin

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of as-prepared Bi4O5Br2 and CDs/Bi4O5Br2 composites; (b) SEM imagine (c) TEM (d, e) HRTEM of 3 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2 composite.

(a) UV–vis DRS of series of CDs/Bi4O5Br2 composites and pure Bi4O5Br2 (The illustration is (aEphoton) 1/2 vs. Ephoton curves of the as-prepared pure Bi4O5Br2 and 3 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2 composites samples) (b) Raman spectra of CDs, Bi4O5Br2 and 3 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2 samples.

(a) Photocatalytic degrading Ciprofloxacin with samples under visible-light irradiation (λ ≥ 420 nm); (b) kinetics of degrading Ciprofloxacin with samples; (c)PL spectra of Bi4O5Br2 and 3 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2 with an excitation wavelength of 325 nm; (d) Possible mechanism for degrading Ciprofloxacin with CDs/Bi4O5Br2 under visible light.

head.ce:title: Solvothermal synthesis of CDs/Bi4O5Br2 nanocomposites with improved visible-light photocatalytic ciprofloxacin (CIP) decontamination

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental and Safety Engineering, Changzhou University, Jiangsu 213164, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Safety Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental and Safety Engineering, Changzhou University, Jiangsu 213164, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) and Bi-riched bismuth oxyhalide have great potential in the field of photocatalysis. Here, a series of CDs/Bi4O5Br2 at different loading ratios were successfully synthesized by solvothermal method. Experiments of visible light degradation of ciprofloxacin (CIP) showed that 3 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2 had the highest activity (5.39 times that of Bi4O5Br2) and the degradation rate reached 98%. The samples were characterized by XRD, SEM, TEM, EDS, Mapping, UV–vis DRS, Raman, and PL spectra. The absorption edge of 3 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2 was red-shifted from 455 nm to 580 nm compared with Bi4O5Br2. The enhancement of photocatalytic activity was mainly due to the fact that the addition of CDs not only broadened the photo-response range, but reduced the recombination rate of photo-induced carriers with good electron transport interfaces formed.

ce:section.ce:para: As a broad-spectrum antibiotic agent, the widespread use of ciprofloxacin (CIP) and the lack of a good treatment process have led to its ubiquitous presence in surface waters, and its constant release into the water environment may accelerate bacterial resistance. Therefore, it is extremely important to find a right way to degrade ciprofloxacin [1]. A large number of scientific practices have proved that photocatalysis has been widely used as a means of degrading ciprofloxacin. Bi-based semiconductor photocatalysts gradually come into people’s sight and have aroused more and more investigate interests for its high visible light photocatalytic performance, structural stability and non-toxicity [2].

BiOX (X = Cl, Br, I) as a representative member of Bi-based semiconductor photocatalysts, has drawn lots of attention owing to its unique layered structure, which makes it have good optical and electrical properties [3]. Unfortunately, the narrow visible light response, and the high photo-generated carrier recombination rate still greatly limit their application. Based on this, series of optimized strategies have been applied to overcome these deficiencies. Recently, Bi-rich strategy has been applied to widen the light absorption range and valence or conduction band position of the BiOX materials to realize a higher photocatalytic activity. Bi4O5Br2 material displays a charming photocatalytic performance for its suitable band gap position and strong response in visible light region [4,5]. However, the single non-stoichiometric ratio of Bi-O-Br is still limited by the higher photogenerated electron-hole pairs recombination rate, which still restricts the photocatalytic activity [6,7]. Not only can Carbon dots (CDs) be used as a separate photocatalyst, but they can as a multifunctional component in composite photocatalysts to provide a broader photoresponse and promote separation of e-/h+ pairs, as well as to stabilize semiconductor photocatalysts [8].

Herein, the novel CDs/Bi4O5Br2 photocatalysts were synthesized in this work. Ciprofloxacin was picked as target pollutant, and the interfaces promoting electron-hole separation between carbon dots and Bi4O5Br2 was investigated.

The CDs were synthesized by top-down approaches, aiming to physically exfoliate large carbon structures according to the literature [9]. The CDs modified Bi4O5Br2 photocatalysts were prepared through a facile hydrothermal treatment. 1 mmol [C16mim]Br (1-hexadecyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide) (99%) and a certain number of CDs were dispersed in 20 mL of mannitol solution (0.1 M) under continuous stirring and marked as solution A. 1 mmol bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O) was added into 20 mL mannitol solution to obtain solution B. Then solution B was added into solution A drop by drop. And NaOH aqueous solution (2 M) was added to adjust the pH value to 11.5. After stirring for 0.5 h, the resulting suspension was transferred into Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclaves (50 mL) and kept at 140 °C for 24 h. The sludge was centrifuged and washed three times with distilled water and ethanol. At last, the product was dried 5 h at 50 °C. For CDs/Bi4O5Br2, The weight of the introduced CDs was 1, 3 and 5 wt%, respectively. Similarly, Bi4O5Br2 was obtained through the same method without adding CDs according to the literature [10].

The structure and properties of the samples were researched by XRD (D/MAX2500 diffractometer) and Raman spectra (LabRAM HR Evolution spectrometer). Investigation on the morphology of the composites was conducted by SEM (SUPRA55) and TEM (HRTEM, JEOL JEM 2100F). The photocatalytic property was studied by UV–vis DRS (Shimadzu UV-2550) and PL spectra (Edinburgh FL/FS900 spectrophotometer).

The photocatalytic experiments were carried out in a photoreaction instrument with a 500 W Xenon lamp and with a 400 nm cutoff filters. 10 mg as-prepared photocatalysts was poured into 50 mL aqueous solution of CIP (10 mg/L). Before light up, the mixed solution was stirred for 1 h in the dark to achieve the absorption–desorption equilibrium. 3.5 mL reaction mixture was sampled regularly and the obtained supernatant by a centrifuge and the concentration of CIP at the maximum absorption peak of 275 nm.

XRD patterns of as-prepared materials were shown in Fig. 1 a. The corresponding (1 1 2), ( −1 1 3), (0 2 0), (4 2 2) and (8 1 1) planes of Bi4O5Br2 were located at 24.31°, 29.54°, 31.86°, 45.58° and 55.38° in XRD pattern, respectively, and the XRD pattern readily indexed to Bi4O5B2 monoclinic phase (JCPDS Card # 97-041-2591). The CDs/Bi4O5Br2 samples had a similar XRD patterns to Bi4O5Br2, indicating that the loading of the CDs had almost no effect on the crystal structure of Bi4O5Br2. No obviously typical peaks of CDs could be found in the XRD patterns of CDs/Bi4O5Br2, which could be ascribed to the low amount and higher dispersity of CDs in the CDs/Bi4O5Br2 [11].

Fig. 1b–e displayed the SEM, TEM and HRTEM image of 3 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2. It could be seen from Fig. 1b–d that the size of CDs was 10–20 nm. It could be seen from Fig. 1d, e that the lattice spacing of Bi4O5Br2 was 0.302 nm [12], which corresponded to its ( −1 1 3) crystal plane, and the CDs with a lattice spacing of 0.2 nm [9] were loaded on the Bi4O5Br2 well. In addition, in Fig.S1, the EDS elemental spectrum clearly presented four elements of Br, Bi, C, and O. In Fig.S2, elemental mapping revealed that each element was homogeneous distribution.

UV–vis DRS was recorded to explore the light-absorption ability of CDs/Bi4O5Br2 and Bi4O5Br2. The absorption edge of pure CDs/Bi4O5Br2 could be determined to be 580 nm from Fig. 2 a, and the variety of red-shifted was 125 nm compared with that of Bi4O5Br2 (455 nm), which proved that the combination of CDs and Bi4O5Br2 improved visible light absorption. The illustration of Fig. 2a indicated that the band gap of 3 wt% CDs/ Bi4O5Br2 (1.76 eV) was significantly lower than that of Bi4O5Br2 (2.41 eV), which could be attributed to the visible light absorption ability of CDs.

Raman technique was employed to investigate the structural changes of the pure Bi4O5Br2 and 3 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2 composite and relevant results were shown in Fig. 2b. The bands located at 93 and 141 cm−1 for pure Bi4O5Br2 were attributed to the A1g internal and E1g internal Bi-Br stretching modes [10], respectively. Two obvious Raman peaks of a D band and G band around 1340 and 1580 cm−1 showed the existence of CDs in 3 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2, which further confirmed that CDs were successfully compounded with the Bi4O5Br2 [13].

The photocatalytic performance of the Bi4O5Br2 and CDs/Bi4O5Br2 was estimated by the photocatalytic degradation of CIP aqueous solution under visible light irradiation. The degradation experiment was conducted three times and the average absorbance of samples was taken. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the self-degradation of Bi4O5Br2 was investigated by self-degradation experiment. It could be concluded that the self-degradation capacity of CIP could be ignored. Among as-prepared CDs/Bi4O5Br2 and Bi4O5Br2 materials, 3 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2 exhibited the highest photocatalytic activity and corresponding degradability value was 98% when the time was 120 min. At the same time, only about 52% CIP could be degraded over pure Bi4O5Br2. As shown in Fig. 3b, the corresponding pseudo-first-order kinetic curves showed that the 3 wt% CDs/Bi4O5Br2 owned the maximum rate constant (K = 0.0313 min−1), 5.39 times that of Bi4O5Br2 (K = 0.0058 min−1).

The peak position from 400 to 600 nm using the excited wavelength of 355 nm was displayed in Fig. 3c. The PL intensity decreased due to doping of CDs, demonstrating the CDs prevented recombination of photoinduced electrons and holes. Fig. 3d showed the interface with strong coupling interaction between carbon dots and Bi4O5Br2, which utilized the excellent electron conductivity of CDs sufficiently. To be specific, CDs captured electrons transferred from the valence band to the conduction band in Bi4O5Br2 under visible light irradiation, which inhibited electron-holes recombination. The holes (h+) on the valence band and electrons (e−) and the conduction band reacted with H2O and O2 to form hydroxyl radicals (·OH) and superoxide radicals (·O2−) respectively, and the organic pollutants were degraded by redox reaction.

In consequence, CDs/Bi4O5Br2 composites were synthesized successfully by solvothermal method. CDs with a size of 10–20 nm were loaded on the Bi4O5Br2. After 120 min, the degradation rate of CIP was up to 98%, which was 46% higher than the activity of pure Bi4O5Br2. The improved photocatalytic activity of CDs/Bi4O5Br2 was due to the interfaces between carbon dots and Bi4O5Br2 as well as the decreased band gap. This research offers an efficient method for combining CDs with Bi4O5Br2 to upon the photocatalytic property of Bi4O5Br2 to degrade CIP.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2019.05.010.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
